<title>Homological algebra</title>
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.css">

<script>
(function(){
  function renderAll(){
    if(typeof renderMathInElement!=='function'){ return setTimeout(renderAll,50); }
    renderMathInElement(document.body,{
      delimiters:[
        {left:'$$',right:'$$',display:true},
        {left:'$',right:'$',display:false},
        {left:'\\(',right:'\\)',display:false},
        {left:'\\[',right:'\\]',display:true}
      ],
      throwOnError:false
    });
  }
  if(document.readyState==='loading') document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded',renderAll);
  else renderAll();
})();
</script>
<style>
  :root{
    --bg:#0f1218; --panel:#161b24; --panel2:#1c2230;
    --ink:#e8ecf1; --mute:#9aa4b2; --line:#2a3242;
    --yellow:#ffd866; --blue:#58c4dd; --green:#83c167;
    --pink:#e07a5f; --violet:#b39ddb; --cyan:#7de0d6;
  }
  *{box-sizing:border-box}
  html,body{margin:0;padding:0;background:var(--bg);color:var(--ink);
    font-family:-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,"Segoe UI",Inter,Roboto,sans-serif;
    font-size:17px;line-height:1.65}
  a{color:var(--blue)}
  code{font-family:ui-monospace,SFMono-Regular,Menlo,Consolas,monospace;
    background:#0b0f16;padding:1px 6px;border-radius:4px;color:#f0c987;font-size:0.92em}
  main{max-width:880px;margin:0 auto;padding:4rem 1.5rem 8rem}
  h1{font-size:2.4rem;letter-spacing:-0.01em;margin:0 0 0.4rem}
  h2{font-size:1.6rem;margin:3.2rem 0 0.8rem;color:#fff;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding-bottom:.4rem}
  h3{font-size:1.15rem;margin:1.8rem 0 .6rem;color:var(--yellow)}
  p{margin:0.7rem 0}
  .sub{color:var(--mute);margin-top:0;margin-bottom:2rem}
  .widget{background:var(--panel);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:10px;
    padding:1rem 1.1rem 1.2rem;margin:1.2rem 0}
  .widget .hd{display:flex;justify-content:space-between;align-items:baseline;margin-bottom:.6rem;gap:.8rem;flex-wrap:wrap}
  .widget .hd .ttl{font-weight:600;color:#fff;font-size:.95rem;letter-spacing:.02em;text-transform:uppercase}
  .widget .hd .hint{color:var(--mute);font-size:.85rem}
  .row{display:flex;gap:.6rem;flex-wrap:wrap;align-items:center;margin:.4rem 0}
  .row label{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem}
  input[type="text"],input[type="number"],select,textarea{
    background:#0b0f16;color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .55rem;font-family:inherit;font-size:.95rem}
  textarea{font-family:ui-monospace,monospace}
  input[type="range"]{accent-color:var(--yellow);flex:1;max-width:260px}
  button{background:var(--panel2);color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);

  button:hover{background:#242c3e}
  button.active{background:var(--yellow);color:#111;border-color:var(--yellow)}

  .note{border-left:3px solid var(--violet);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d8d2ef;background:#1a1828;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}

  svg{display:block;margin:0 auto;max-width:100%}

  nav.toc{position:sticky;top:0;background:rgba(15,18,24,.93);backdrop-filter:blur(8px);

    display:flex;gap:1rem;flex-wrap:wrap;font-size:.9rem}
  nav.toc a{color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none}
  nav.toc a:hover{color:var(--yellow)}
  .hero{padding:3rem 0 1rem}
  .small{font-size:.88rem;color:var(--mute)}
  footer{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem;text-align:center;margin-top:4rem}

  /* sticky sidebar TOC (wide screens only; narrow screens fall back to top-nav) */
  aside.sidetoc{position:fixed;top:4.2rem;
    left:max(1rem, calc(50% - 440px - 220px));
    width:200px;max-height:calc(100vh - 6rem);overflow-y:auto;
    padding-right:1rem;border-right:1px solid var(--line);
    font-size:.88rem;z-index:5}
  aside.sidetoc .sidetoc-title{font-size:.7rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;

  aside.sidetoc ol{list-style:none;padding:0;margin:0}

    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;

    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;

  aside.callback ul li{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.callback a{color:inherit}

    margin:2rem 0 1rem;padding:.6rem .9rem;
    background:rgba(0,0,0,0.22);
    border-radius:6px;
    font-size:.85rem;

  details.changelog summary{
    cursor:pointer;letter-spacing:.1em;text-transform:uppercase;
    font-size:.7rem;color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);

  details.changelog table{margin-top:.5rem;border-collapse:collapse;width:100%;font-size:.82rem}

  details.changelog td:first-child{color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);white-space:nowrap;font-variant-numeric:tabular-nums}

    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 6%, transparent);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 55%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.related .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.related > div{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.related .more{color:var(--mute);font-style:italic}
  aside.related a{color:inherit}
</style>

Homological algebra, from chain complexes to spectral sequences

The machinery that measures failure of exactness. Chain complexes, exact sequences, the snake lemma, Ext and Tor, resolutions, and a gentle taste of spectral sequences.

blocks.html:
1. Chain complexes and homology

A chain complex of abelian groups (or $A$-modules) is a sequence

$\cdots \to C_{n+1} \xrightarrow{\partial_{n+1}} C_n \xrightarrow{\partial_n} C_{n-1} \to \cdots$

such that $\partial_n\circ\partial_{n+1}=0$ for every $n$. Equivalently, $\operatorname{im}\partial_{n+1}\subseteq\ker\partial_n$. The $n$-th homology

$H_n(C_\bullet) \;=\; \ker\partial_n\,/\,\operatorname{im}\partial_{n+1}$

measures exactly how far this containment is from being equality. Elements of $\ker\partial$ are cycles; elements of $\operatorname{im}\partial$ are boundaries. A cycle that is not a boundary records a "real" hole.

The archetype: given a simplicial complex $X$, let $C_n$ be the free abelian group on the $n$-simplices of $X$ and let $\partial$ send an $n$-simplex to the alternating sum of its codimension-$1$ faces. Then $\partial^2=0$ is a combinatorial calculation, and the resulting $H_n(X;\mathbb{Z})$ are the simplicial homology groups of $X$. Over $\mathbb{Q}$ (which is what the widget below uses, so that everything reduces to linear algebra) the Betti numbers are $b_n = \dim_\mathbb{Q} H_n$.

Chain complex calculator
pick a preset or enter boundary matrices · computes $H_0, H_1, H_2$ over $\mathbb{Q}$

Try the sphere: it has $H_0=\mathbb{Q}$ (connected), $H_1=0$ (no 1-dimensional holes), $H_2=\mathbb{Q}$ (one enclosed void). The torus famously has $H_0=\mathbb{Q},\ H_1=\mathbb{Q}^2,\ H_2=\mathbb{Q}$ — two independent $1$-cycles (the two generating loops) and one $2$-cycle (the whole surface). Ranks of homology are topological invariants; they do not depend on the triangulation.

See also
  • Ideals and quotient rings
Used in
Commutative algebra · Flatness and Tor
Homological algebra · Derived functors and Ext/Tor
Homological algebra · Exact sequences
</section>

<script>
/* =============================== SECTION 1: Chain complexes =============================== */
(function(){
  // preset data: dims = [dim C_0, dim C_1, dim C_2], d1 (int matrix), d2 (int matrix or null)
  const PRESETS = {
    triangle: {
      name:'triangle boundary',
      dims:[3,3,0],
      d1:[[-1,0,1],[1,-1,0],[0,1,-1]],
      d2:null,
      note:'3 vertices, 3 edges, no faces. H_0 = Q, H_1 = Q, H_2 = 0. Same as a circle.'
    },
    disk: {
      name:'filled triangle (disk)',
      dims:[3,3,1],
      d1:[[-1,0,1],[1,-1,0],[0,1,-1]],
      d2:[[1],[1],[1]],
      note:'Same boundary as the triangle, but now with a face that fills it in. H_0 = Q, H_1 = 0, H_2 = 0. Contractible.'
    },
    s1:{
      name:'circle S^1 (two-edge model)',
      dims:[2,2,0],
      d1:[[-1,-1],[1,1]],
      d2:null,
      note:'Two vertices A, B; two edges e1, e2 from A to B. H_0 = Q (connected), H_1 = Q (the cycle e1 - e2).'
    },
    s2:{
      name:'S^2 = ∂Δ^3',
      dims:[4,6,4],
      // vertices 1,2,3,4; edges: 12,13,14,23,24,34
      // ∂(ij) = j - i
      d1:[
        [-1,-1,-1, 0, 0, 0], // v1
        [ 1, 0, 0,-1,-1, 0], // v2
        [ 0, 1, 0, 1, 0,-1], // v3
        [ 0, 0, 1, 0, 1, 1], // v4
      ],
      // faces: 123, 124, 134, 234; edge order as above
      // ∂(ijk) = jk - ik + ij
      d2:[
        [ 1, 1, 0, 0], // 12
        [-1, 0, 1, 0], // 13
        [ 0,-1,-1, 0], // 14
        [ 1, 0, 0, 1], // 23
        [ 0, 1, 0,-1], // 24
        [ 0, 0, 1, 1], // 34
      ],
      note:'The boundary of a tetrahedron: 4 vertices, 6 edges, 4 triangular faces. Expect H_0 = Q, H_1 = 0, H_2 = Q.'
    },
    torus:{
      name:'torus (7-vertex triangulation)',
      // Uses Möbius 7-vertex minimal triangulation; we bake in the precomputed boundary matrices.
      // However, constructing these by hand is error-prone. Instead we use a coarser "square with opposite edges identified" ∆-complex: 1 vertex, 2 edges, 1 face, but that's not simplicial. Use a still-coarse CW-style but with ∂^2 = 0 honest.
      // Use: C_0 = Q (one vertex v), C_1 = Q^2 (loops a, b), C_2 = Q (one 2-cell T with boundary a + b - a - b = 0).
      dims:[1,2,1],
      d1:[[0,0]],
      d2:[[0],[0]],
      note:'∆-complex model of the torus: one vertex, two loops a,b, one 2-cell attached along aba^{-1}b^{-1} whose homological boundary is zero. H_0 = Q, H_1 = Q^2, H_2 = Q.'
    },
    disconnected:{
      name:'two disjoint edges',
      dims:[4,2,0],
      d1:[[-1,0],[1,0],[0,-1],[0,1]],
      d2:null,
      note:'Two components, no cycles. H_0 = Q^2, H_1 = 0, H_2 = 0.'
    },
    custom:{
      name:'custom',
      dims:[2,2,1],
      d1:[[-1,-1],[1,1]],
      d2:[[1],[-1]],
      note:'Edit the matrices; the widget will check d_1 ∘ d_2 = 0.'
    }
  };

  const pick = $('#cc-pick'), d1ta = $('#cc-d1'), d2ta = $('#cc-d2'),
        go = $('#cc-go'), out = $('#cc-out'), status = $('#cc-status'), dimsDiv = $('#cc-dims');

  function parseMatrix(str){
    const rows = str.trim().split(/\n+/).map(r=>r.trim()).filter(Boolean);
    if(rows.length===0) return [];
    return rows.map(r=>r.split(/[\s,]+/).filter(Boolean).map(Number));
  }
  function matToStr(M){
    if(!M || M.length===0) return '';
    return M.map(r=>r.join(' ')).join('\n');
  }
  function loadPreset(key){
    const P = PRESETS[key];
    d1ta.value = matToStr(P.d1);
    d2ta.value = matToStr(P.d2);
    dimsDiv.textContent = `dim C_0 = ${P.dims[0]},  dim C_1 = ${P.dims[1]},  dim C_2 = ${P.dims[2]}`;
    status.textContent = P.note;
    status.style.color = 'var(--mute)';
  }

  function compute(){
    let d1raw = parseMatrix(d1ta.value);
    let d2raw = parseMatrix(d2ta.value);
    // Infer dims
    const c0 = d1raw.length ? d1raw.length : 0;
    const c1 = d1raw.length ? d1raw[0].length : (d2raw.length ? d2raw.length : 0);
    const c2 = d2raw.length ? d2raw[0].length : 0;
    // consistency check
    if(d1raw.length>0){
      for(const r of d1raw) if(r.length!==c1){ bad('d_1 rows have inconsistent length'); return; }
    }
    if(d2raw.length>0){
      for(const r of d2raw) if(r.length!==c2){ bad('d_2 rows have inconsistent length'); return; }
      if(d2raw.length !== c1){ bad(`d_2 has ${d2raw.length} rows but C_1 has dimension ${c1}`); return; }
    }
    const D1 = d1raw.length? matFromInt(d1raw) : [];
    const D2 = d2raw.length? matFromInt(d2raw) : [];

    // ranks
    const rank_d1 = D1.length? matRank(D1) : 0;
    const rank_d2 = D2.length? matRank(D2) : 0;
    // ∂^2 check: only makes sense if both present
    let d2check = 'n/a';
    if(D1.length && D2.length){
      const prod = matMul(D1, D2);
      d2check = matIsZero(prod) ? 'yes' : 'NO — d_1 ∘ d_2 ≠ 0';
    }

    // dimensions of C_n
    const dim0 = c0, dim1 = c1, dim2 = c2;

2. Exact sequences

A sequence of abelian groups and homomorphisms

$\cdots \to A \xrightarrow{f} B \xrightarrow{g} C \to \cdots$

is exact at $B$ if $\operatorname{im} f = \ker g$. Exactness is a strengthening of "$g\circ f = 0$" (which says $\operatorname{im} f \subseteq \ker g$): the other inclusion is the content.

The two fundamental special cases:

  • Short exact sequence. $0 \to A \xrightarrow{f} B \xrightarrow{g} C \to 0$ — i.e. $f$ is injective, $g$ is surjective, and $\operatorname{im} f = \ker g$. Equivalently: $A\cong\ker g \subseteq B$ and $C\cong B/A$.
  • Long exact sequence. An exact sequence stretching in both directions. We'll see in §3 how a short exact sequence of complexes produces a long exact sequence in homology — the single most frequently used tool in the subject.
Classic example: for any $n\ge 1$, $$0 \;\to\; \mathbb{Z} \;\xrightarrow{\;\cdot n\;}\; \mathbb{Z} \;\twoheadrightarrow\; \mathbb{Z}/n \;\to\; 0.$$ Multiplication by $n$ is injective (no torsion in $\mathbb{Z}$), and its cokernel is $\mathbb{Z}/n$. Apply a left-exact functor like $\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathbb{Z})$ and you discover that $\operatorname{Ext}^1(\mathbb{Z}/n,\mathbb{Z})\cong\mathbb{Z}/n$ — a failure of exactness precisely measured by $\operatorname{Ext}$.

3. The snake lemma

Everyone meets the snake lemma once and then uses it forever. Suppose we have a commutative diagram of abelian groups with exact rows:

$\begin{array}{ccccccccc} 0 & \to & A & \xrightarrow{\;i\;} & B & \xrightarrow{\;p\;} & C & \to & 0 \\ & & \downarrow\alpha & & \downarrow\beta & & \downarrow\gamma & & \\ 0 & \to & A' & \xrightarrow{\;i'\;} & B' & \xrightarrow{\;p'\;} & C' & \to & 0. \end{array}$

Snake lemma. There is a natural connecting homomorphism $\delta:\ker\gamma\to\operatorname{coker}\alpha$ making the following sequence exact: $$0 \to \ker\alpha \to \ker\beta \to \ker\gamma \xrightarrow{\;\delta\;} \operatorname{coker}\alpha \to \operatorname{coker}\beta \to \operatorname{coker}\gamma \to 0.$$

The point is that $\delta$ exists at all. Given $c\in\ker\gamma\subseteq C$, you lift to $b\in B$ with $p(b)=c$, apply $\beta$ to get $b'\in B'$; commutativity forces $p'(b') = \gamma(c) = 0$, so $b'$ lies in $\ker p' = \operatorname{im} i'$, and we can pull back to a unique $a'\in A'$. Well-defined modulo $\operatorname{im}\alpha$ by construction. The "snake" is the path your finger draws through the diagram as you follow the recipe: right, down, left. That is exactly the connecting morphism.

Specializing the snake lemma to a short exact sequence of chain complexes — interpret the top and bottom rows as the same complex at two consecutive degrees and iterate — gives the long exact sequence in homology. This is the single most important consequence.

Trace the connecting morphism δ
concrete example: the SES $0\to\mathbb{Z}/2\to\mathbb{Z}/4\to\mathbb{Z}/2\to 0$ mapping to itself

In the snake lemma's diagram, the rows are the SES at homological degree $n$ (top) and degree $n-1$ (bottom); the vertical maps are the differentials. Then $\ker\gamma/\operatorname{im}\alpha$ pieces are precisely $H_n(C)$-parts, and $\delta$ becomes the boundary in the long exact sequence in homology.

4. The five lemma

The snake lemma's close cousin, used constantly to recognize isomorphisms inside big diagrams. Suppose we have a commutative diagram of abelian groups (or objects of any abelian category) with exact rows:

$\begin{array}{ccccccccc} A_1 & \to & A_2 & \to & A_3 & \to & A_4 & \to & A_5 \\ \downarrow f_1 & & \downarrow f_2 & & \downarrow f_3 & & \downarrow f_4 & & \downarrow f_5 \\ B_1 & \to & B_2 & \to & B_3 & \to & B_4 & \to & B_5. \end{array}$

Five lemma. If $f_1, f_2, f_4, f_5$ are isomorphisms then $f_3$ is also an isomorphism. More precisely, it splits into two halves: $f_2$ surjective + $f_4$ injective $\Rightarrow$ $f_3$ surjective; $f_2$ injective + $f_4$ surjective $\Rightarrow$ $f_3$ injective.

The proof is the archetypal diagram chase. To see $f_3$ is injective, suppose $f_3(a_3) = 0$. Push $a_3$ one step to the right; by commutativity its image under $f_4$ is also zero, and since $f_4$ is injective, the pushed element is zero. Exactness then writes $a_3$ as the image of some $a_2$; push $a_2$ down and use commutativity; exactness on the bottom row backs up $f_2(a_2)$ to a $b_1$; the surjectivity of $f_1$ lifts $b_1$ to an $a_1$; finally commutativity forces the image to match. Trace out the tree of implications — it looks like the whale on a Moby-Dick cover.

Five-lemma chase
click step to watch the chase; pick which half to trace

Weakening any hypothesis in the five lemma breaks it: standard counterexamples drop $f_1$ surjective (so $f_3$ can fail to be injective even if everything else is an isomorphism), etc. The four outer hypotheses are each doing work — this is why the lemma is "tight."

5. The long exact sequence in homology

Suppose $0 \to A_\bullet \xrightarrow{f} B_\bullet \xrightarrow{g} C_\bullet \to 0$ is a short exact sequence of chain complexes — a triple of complexes with a chain map of each, so that in every degree $n$ the rows $0\to A_n \to B_n \to C_n\to 0$ are exact and all squares commute with the differentials. Applying the snake lemma to two consecutive degrees extracts a connecting homomorphism $\partial : H_n(C) \to H_{n-1}(A)$, and iterating over every $n$ splices the pieces into one infinite exact sequence:

$\cdots \to H_n(A) \xrightarrow{f_*} H_n(B) \xrightarrow{g_*} H_n(C) \xrightarrow{\;\partial\;} H_{n-1}(A) \to \cdots$

This is the tool you reach for first. For a pair $(X,Y)\hookrightarrow X$ of spaces the short exact sequence of singular-chain complexes $0\to C_\bullet(Y)\to C_\bullet(X)\to C_\bullet(X,Y)\to 0$ produces the relative homology long exact sequence; for $\mathrm{Hom}(-,N)$ applied to a short exact sequence $0\to A\to B\to C\to 0$ the analogous long exact sequence threads $\mathrm{Ext}^i$ together. Every time you see $\operatorname{Ext}^1$ measuring the failure of some $\operatorname{Hom}$ to be surjective, a long exact sequence is doing the bookkeeping.

Mayer–Vietoris is the long exact sequence attached to the SES $0\to C_\bullet(U\cap V)\to C_\bullet(U)\oplus C_\bullet(V)\to C_\bullet(U\cup V)\to 0$. It computes $H_*(S^n)$ in one line: gluing two $n$-disks along an equatorial $S^{n-1}$ gives $H_n(S^n)\cong H_{n-1}(S^{n-1})$, hence inductively $H_n(S^n)\cong\mathbb{Z}$.

6. Projective modules

A module $P$ over a ring $R$ is projective if every surjection $M\twoheadrightarrow P$ splits — equivalently, if for every surjection $\varphi\colon M\twoheadrightarrow N$ and every map $f\colon P\to N$, there exists a lift $\tilde f\colon P\to M$ with $\varphi\circ\tilde f = f$:

$\begin{array}{ccc} & & P \\ & \swarrow_{\tilde f} & \downarrow f \\ M & \xrightarrow{\;\varphi\;\;} & N. \end{array}$

Equivalent characterizations of $P$ projective:
  • $P$ is a direct summand of a free module;
  • $\operatorname{Hom}(P, -)$ is an exact functor (not merely left exact);
  • every short exact sequence $0\to A\to B\to P\to 0$ splits.
Over a PID (including $\mathbb{Z}$), projective $\iff$ free. Over a Dedekind domain, projective rank-$1$ modules are classified by the ideal class group.

Why care? Projectives are exactly the modules whose $\operatorname{Hom}$ functor preserves exactness — they are the "good" source side for derived functors. Free modules are visibly projective (the lift is computed on a basis), and the step from free to projective is exactly "direct summand of free."

7. Injective modules

Dual to projective: a module $I$ is injective if every injection $A\hookrightarrow B$ and every map $f\colon A\to I$ extends to a map $\tilde f\colon B\to I$:

$\begin{array}{ccc} A & \hookrightarrow & B \\ \downarrow f & \swarrow_{\tilde f} & \\ I. & & \end{array}$

Baer's criterion. $I$ is injective over a ring $R$ iff for every ideal $\mathfrak{a}\subseteq R$, every homomorphism $\mathfrak{a}\to I$ extends to $R\to I$. This reduces an a priori huge check to a tractable one — you only have to test against ideals, not all modules.

Over $\mathbb{Z}$, Baer's criterion gives a concrete characterization: a $\mathbb{Z}$-module $I$ is injective iff $I$ is divisible — for every $a\in I$ and every $n\ge 1$, there exists $x\in I$ with $nx=a$. The proof is one line: an ideal of $\mathbb{Z}$ is $(n)$; extending $(n)\to I$ sending $n\mapsto a$ to $\mathbb{Z}\to I$ means finding $x\in I$ with $nx=a$. Examples of injective abelian groups: $\mathbb{Q}$, $\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}$, the Prüfer groups $\mathbb{Z}/p^\infty$. Non-examples: $\mathbb{Z}$, $\mathbb{Z}/n$.

<script>
/* =============================== SECTION 7: Injective / Baer toggle =============================== */
(function(){
  const Isel = $('#inj-I'), nIn = $('#inj-n'), aIn = $('#inj-a'), out = $('#inj-out');

  function gcd2(a,b){a=Math.abs(a);b=Math.abs(b);while(b){[a,b]=[b,a%b]}return a||1}
  function parseFrac(s){
    s = s.trim();
    if(!s) return null;
    if(s.includes('/')){
      const [p,q] = s.split('/').map(x=>parseInt(x.trim(),10));
      if(isNaN(p)||isNaN(q)||q===0) return null;
      return {p,q};
    }
    const v = parseInt(s,10);
    if(isNaN(v)) return null;
    return {p:v,q:1};
  }

  function render(){
    const Ikey = Isel.value;
    const n = Math.max(1, Math.min(12, parseInt(nIn.value,10)||1));
    nIn.value = n;
    const a = aIn.value;
    const lines = [];

    const divisible = {
      Q:true, QmodZ:true, Zpinf:true,
      Z:false, Z4:false, Z2xZ3:false
    };
    const Iname = {
      Q:'ℚ', QmodZ:'ℚ/ℤ', Zpinf:'ℤ/2^∞', Z:'ℤ', Z4:'ℤ/4', Z2xZ3:'ℤ/2 ⊕ ℤ/3'
    }[Ikey];

    lines.push(`I = ${Iname}`);
    lines.push(`ideal tested: (${n}) ⊆ ℤ`);
    lines.push(`targeted a ∈ I: ${a}`);
    lines.push('');

    let ok = false, why = '';
    if(Ikey==='Q'){
      const f = parseFrac(a);
      if(!f){ why = 'parse error; enter a fraction or integer'; }
      else { ok = true; why = `x = ${f.p}/${f.q*n} works; ℚ is divisible.`; }
    } else if(Ikey==='QmodZ'){
      const f = parseFrac(a);
      if(!f){ why = 'parse error; enter a fraction'; }
      else { ok = true; why = `In ℚ/ℤ: x = (${f.p}/${f.q})/${n} = ${f.p}/${f.q*n} (mod 1) works; ℚ/ℤ is divisible.`; }
    } else if(Ikey==='Zpinf'){
      const f = parseFrac(a);
      if(!f){ why = 'parse error'; }
      else {
        let m = n, s = 0;
        while(m%2===0){ m/=2; s++; }
        ok = true;
        why = `ℤ/2^∞ is divisible by every nonzero integer; solving ${n}x = ${f.p}/${f.q} reduces to dividing by the power-of-2 part ${Math.pow(2,s)} (shift the denominator exponent) and by the odd cofactor ${m} (invert mod 2^∞).`;
      }
    } else if(Ikey==='Z'){
      const av = parseInt(a,10);
      if(isNaN(av)){ why='parse error; ℤ elements are integers'; }
      else {
        ok = (av % n === 0);
        why = ok
          ? `x = ${av/n} ∈ ℤ works.`
          : `${n} ∤ ${av}, so no x ∈ ℤ with ${n}x = ${av}. ℤ is NOT divisible, hence NOT injective.`;
      }
    } else if(Ikey==='Z4'){
      const av = parseInt(a,10);
      if(isNaN(av)){ why='parse error; enter an integer mod 4'; }
      else {
        const am = ((av%4)+4)%4;
        const g = gcd2(n,4);
        ok = (am % g === 0);
        why = ok
          ? `In ℤ/4: ${n}x ≡ ${am} mod 4 is solvable (gcd(${n},4)=${g} divides ${am}).`
          : `In ℤ/4: ${n}x ≡ ${am} mod 4 is NOT solvable (gcd(${n},4)=${g} does not divide ${am}). ℤ/4 is NOT divisible.`;
      }
    } else if(Ikey==='Z2xZ3'){
      const av = parseInt(a,10);
      if(isNaN(av)){ why='parse error; enter an integer interpreted mod 6'; }
      else {
        const am = ((av%6)+6)%6;
        const a2 = am%2, a3 = am%3;
        const g2 = gcd2(n,2), g3 = gcd2(n,3);
        const ok2 = (a2 % g2 === 0), ok3 = (a3 % g3 === 0);
        ok = ok2 && ok3;
        why = ok
          ? `Solvable in both factors.`
          : `Fails in factor${!ok2 && !ok3?'s':''}: ${!ok2?'ℤ/2 component (gcd('+n+',2)='+g2+' ∤ '+a2+')':''}${(!ok2&&!ok3)?' and ':''}${!ok3?'ℤ/3 component (gcd('+n+',3)='+g3+' ∤ '+a3+')':''}. Not divisible.`;
      }
    }

    lines.push(`solvable for this instance? ${ok?'YES':'NO'}`);
    lines.push(why);
    lines.push('');
    lines.push(`global verdict: ${divisible[Ikey]?'I is divisible ⇒ injective over ℤ ✓':'I is NOT divisible ⇒ NOT injective over ℤ ✗'}`);

    out.textContent = lines.join('\n');
    out.style.color = divisible[Ikey] ? 'var(--ink)' : (ok ? 'var(--ink)' : 'var(--pink)');
  }

  Isel.addEventListener('change', render);
  nIn.addEventListener('input', render);
  aIn.addEventListener('input', render);
  render();
})();
</script>

8. Flat modules

A module $M$ is flat if the functor $-\otimes_R M$ is exact — equivalently (since $-\otimes M$ is always right exact), if it takes injective maps to injective maps. Flatness is strictly between "free" and nothing: every projective module is flat, but many flat modules are not projective.

The canonical flat modules.
  • Free / projective. $R$, $R^n$, and direct summands of free modules are all flat.
  • Localizations. For any multiplicative set $S\subseteq R$, the localization $S^{-1}R$ is flat over $R$ — tensoring is just localizing on the nose, and localization is exact. Concretely, $\mathbb{Q}$ is flat (but not projective) over $\mathbb{Z}$.
  • Over a PID, flat $\iff$ torsion-free. So $\mathbb{Z}[1/p]$, $\mathbb{Q}$, and $\mathbb{Z}$ are flat; $\mathbb{Z}/n$ is not.

Flatness is detected by $\operatorname{Tor}$: $M$ is flat iff $\operatorname{Tor}_1^R(M,N) = 0$ for every $N$ (and then all higher Tor's vanish too). So "how non-flat is $M$?" is measured by its $\operatorname{Tor}_1$ with all test modules — a quantitative refinement of a qualitative property.

Tensor a SES — watch exactness (or its failure)

9. Derived functors, Ext and Tor

Many constructions in algebra are left or right exact but fail to be exact on both sides. $\operatorname{Hom}(M,-)$ is left exact; $-\otimes_A N$ is right exact; $(-)^G$ (invariants under a group action) is left exact; $\Gamma$ (global sections of a sheaf) is left exact. Derived functors package the failure into a sequence of functors $R^iF$ or $L_iF$ forming, for each short exact sequence in the source, a long exact sequence of derived values.

The recipe: to compute $R^iF(M)$ (for $F$ left exact) choose an injective resolution $0\to M\to I^0\to I^1\to\cdots$, apply $F$, and take cohomology of the resulting complex $F(I^\bullet)$. Dually, for $L_iF$ (with $F$ right exact), use a projective resolution. The miracle: the answer is independent of the resolution.

The two stars:

$\operatorname{Ext}^i_A(M,N)$ is the $i$-th right derived functor of $\operatorname{Hom}_A(-,N)$ applied to $M$ (or equivalently of $\operatorname{Hom}_A(M,-)$ applied to $N$). Compute it as $H^i(\operatorname{Hom}_A(P_\bullet, N))$ for any projective resolution $P_\bullet\to M$.

$\operatorname{Tor}_i^A(M,N)$ is the $i$-th left derived functor of $-\otimes_A N$ applied to $M$. Compute it as $H_i(P_\bullet \otimes_A N)$ for any projective (equivalently flat) resolution $P_\bullet\to M$.

Over $A=\mathbb{Z}$ everything is especially clean. Every finitely generated abelian group $M$ has a two-step free resolution $0\to\mathbb{Z}^r\to\mathbb{Z}^s\to M\to 0$, because the subgroup $\ker$ of a finitely generated free abelian group is again free. Consequently $\mathbb{Z}$ has global dimension $1$: $\operatorname{Ext}^i = \operatorname{Tor}_i = 0$ for $i\ge 2$.

<script>
/* =============================== SECTION 4: Ext and Tor =============================== */
(function(){
  // Parse group spec into list of summands, each an int: 0 means Z, m>0 means Z/m.
  const SPECS = {
    'Z':[0], 'Z2':[2], 'Z4':[4], 'Z6':[6],
    'ZxZ':[0,0], 'Z2xZ3':[2,3], 'Z4xZ':[4,0],
    'QmodZ':[{qz:true}],
    'Q':[{q:true}]
  };
  const NAMES = {
    'Z':'ℤ', 'Z2':'ℤ/2', 'Z4':'ℤ/4', 'Z6':'ℤ/6',
    'ZxZ':'ℤ ⊕ ℤ', 'Z2xZ3':'ℤ/2 ⊕ ℤ/3', 'Z4xZ':'ℤ/4 ⊕ ℤ',
    'QmodZ':'ℚ/ℤ', 'Q':'ℚ'
  };

  // Tor_i^Z and Ext^i_Z are bilinear (distribute over direct sums). We implement on each pair of summands and add up.
  // For each summand pair (s_M, s_N):
  //   Tor_0(M,N) = M ⊗ N. Ext^0 = Hom(M,N).
  //   Tor_1(M,N)                Ext^1(M,N)
  //   Z,Z                : ⊗ = Z       Hom = Z
  //   Z,Z/n              : ⊗ = Z/n     Hom = Z/n                (only cyclic images possible)
  //   Z/m, Z             : ⊗ = Z/m     Hom = 0
  //   Z/m, Z/n           : ⊗ = Z/gcd   Hom = Z/gcd
  //   Tor_1:
  //   Z,anything : 0 (Z is flat)
  //   anything,Z : 0 (Z is flat)
  //   Z/m, Z/n : Z/gcd(m,n)
  //   Ext^1:
  //   Z, anything : 0 (Z is projective)
  //   Z/m, Z      : Z/m
  //   Z/m, Z/n    : Z/gcd(m,n)
  //   Special (rationals):
  //   Q is flat and divisible ⇒ Tor_i(-,Q) = 0 for i≥1, Ext^i(Z/m, Q) = 0.
  //   Q/Z is divisible ⇒ injective over Z. Ext^i(-,Q/Z) = 0 for i≥1 (so Hom(-,Q/Z) is exact; this is the Pontryagin dual for finite groups). Tor_i(M,Q/Z) detects torsion: Tor_1(M,Q/Z) = torsion(M).
  //   Tor_1(Z, Q/Z) = 0, Tor_1(Z/m, Q/Z) = Z/m, Tor_0(Z, Q/Z) = Q/Z, Tor_0(Z/m, Q/Z) = Z/m.
  //   Hom(Z, Q/Z) = Q/Z, Hom(Z/m, Q/Z) = Z/m.
  //   Hom(Z,Q)=Q, Hom(Z/m,Q)=0.

  function summandDesc(s){
    if(typeof s === 'number') return s===0? 'ℤ' : `ℤ/${s}`;
    if(s.qz) return 'ℚ/ℤ';
    if(s.q) return 'ℚ';
    return '?';
  }
  function isZ(s){ return typeof s==='number' && s===0; }
  function isZn(s){ return typeof s==='number' && s>0; }

  // returns invariant factor list for the resulting abelian group (0 = Z summand), plus a Q-dim if Q appears
  function pairResult(sM, sN, which){
    // which ∈ {'tensor','hom','tor1','ext1'}
    // All pairs involving Q or Q/Z handled as special cases.
    const ret = {ints:[], qDim:0, qmodZ:0}; // qmodZ: integer #copies of Q/Z

    function factor(m,n){ return gcd(m,n); }

    // Rational or Q/Z shortcuts
    if(sM && sM.q){ // M = Q
      if(which==='tensor'){ // Q ⊗ N
        if(isZ(sN)) ret.qDim=1;
        else if(isZn(sN)) { /* Q ⊗ Z/n = 0 */ }
        else if(sN && sN.q) ret.qDim=1;
        else if(sN && sN.qz) { /* Q ⊗ Q/Z = 0 */ }
        return ret;
      }
      if(which==='hom'){ // Hom(Q, N)
        if(isZ(sN)) { /* Hom(Q,Z) = 0 */ return ret; }
        if(isZn(sN)) { return ret; }
        if(sN && sN.q){ ret.qDim=1; return ret; }
        if(sN && sN.qz){ // Hom(Q, Q/Z) — huge; skip or mark
          ret.special='Hom(ℚ,ℚ/ℤ) (uncountable; not fin. gen.)';
          return ret;
        }
      }
      if(which==='tor1' || which==='ext1'){ /* Q is flat AND injective as abelian group, so Tor=Ext=0 */ return ret; }
    }
    if(sN && sN.q){ // N = Q
      if(which==='tensor'){
        if(isZ(sM)) ret.qDim=1;
        else if(isZn(sM)) {}
        return ret;
      }
      if(which==='hom'){
        if(isZ(sM)) ret.qDim=1;
        else if(isZn(sM)) {}
        return ret;
      }
      if(which==='tor1' || which==='ext1') return ret;
    }
    if(sM && sM.qz){ // M = Q/Z
      if(which==='tensor'){ // (Q/Z) ⊗ N
        if(isZ(sN)) ret.qmodZ=1;
        else if(isZn(sN)) ret.ints.push(sN);
        return ret;
      }
      if(which==='hom'){ // Hom(Q/Z, N) — usually huge; over finite N it is 0 for Z/n; for Q/Z it's huge (≅ Ẑ)
        if(isZn(sN)) return ret;
        ret.special = 'Hom(ℚ/ℤ, ·) (non-finitely-generated)';
        return ret;
      }
      if(which==='tor1'){
        if(isZ(sN)) return ret;
        if(isZn(sN)) { ret.ints.push(sN); return ret; }
        return ret;
      }
      if(which==='ext1'){ return ret; /* Q/Z injective */ }
    }
    if(sN && sN.qz){ // N = Q/Z
      if(which==='tensor'){
        if(isZ(sM)) ret.qmodZ=1;
        else if(isZn(sM)) ret.ints.push(sM);
        return ret;
      }
      if(which==='hom'){
        if(isZ(sM)) ret.qmodZ=1;
        else if(isZn(sM)) ret.ints.push(sM);
        return ret;
      }
      if(which==='tor1'){
        if(isZ(sM)) return ret;
        if(isZn(sM)) { ret.ints.push(sM); return ret; }
        return ret;
      }
      if(which==='ext1'){ return ret; }
    }

    // now only Z, Z/n pairs
    if(isZ(sM) && isZ(sN)){
      if(which==='tensor' || which==='hom') ret.ints.push(0);
      return ret;
    }
    if(isZ(sM) && isZn(sN)){
      if(which==='tensor' || which==='hom') ret.ints.push(sN);
      return ret;
    }
    if(isZn(sM) && isZ(sN)){
      if(which==='tensor') ret.ints.push(sM);
      if(which==='ext1') ret.ints.push(sM);
      return ret;
    }
    if(isZn(sM) && isZn(sN)){
      const g = factor(sM, sN);
      if(which==='tensor' || which==='hom' || which==='tor1' || which==='ext1'){
        if(g>1) ret.ints.push(g);
      }
      return ret;
    }
    return ret;
  }

  function combine(specM, specN, which){
    const total = {ints:[], qDim:0, qmodZ:0, special:null};
    for(const a of specM){
      for(const b of specN){
        const r = pairResult(a,b,which);
        total.ints.push(...r.ints);
        total.qDim += r.qDim || 0;
        total.qmodZ += r.qmodZ || 0;
        if(r.special) total.special = r.special;
      }
    }
    return total;
  }
  function formatResult(R){
    if(R.special) return R.special;
    const parts = [];
    // free
    const free = R.ints.filter(x=>x===0).length;
    if(free>0) parts.push(free===1?'ℤ':`ℤ^${free}`);
    // cyclic
    const cyc = R.ints.filter(x=>x>0).sort((a,b)=>a-b);
    for(const c of cyc) parts.push(`ℤ/${c}`);
    if(R.qDim>0) parts.push(R.qDim===1?'ℚ':`ℚ^${R.qDim}`);
    if(R.qmodZ>0) parts.push(R.qmodZ===1?'ℚ/ℤ':`(ℚ/ℤ)^${R.qmodZ}`);
    if(parts.length===0) return '0';
    return parts.join(' ⊕ ');
  }

  // Describe free resolution for M:
  function resolutionDesc(specM, Mname){
    // For summands (0,0,0,... and m1,m2,...), resolution is
    //   0 → Z^r → Z^s → M → 0
    // with s = number of summands, r = number of finite cyclic summands, and
    // the middle matrix is diag(m_1,...,m_k,0,...,0).
    const r = specM.filter(x=>typeof x==='number' && x>0).length;
    const s = specM.filter(x=>typeof x==='number').length;
    const mi = specM.filter(x=>typeof x==='number' && x>0);
    const hasSpecial = specM.some(x=>typeof x!=='number');
    if(hasSpecial){
      // Q and Q/Z are not finitely generated; skip honest resolution
      return `(${Mname} is not finitely generated over ℤ — skipping explicit resolution.)`;
    }
    const lines = [];
    lines.push(`Free resolution of ${Mname}:`);
    if(r===0){
      lines.push(`   0 → ℤ^${s} → ${Mname} → 0    (${Mname} is free)`);
    } else {
      const diag = mi.map(m=>`${m}`).join(', ') + (s>r? ', 0…0' : '');
      lines.push(`   0 → ℤ^${r} → ℤ^${s} → ${Mname} → 0`);
      lines.push(`   middle map given by diagonal matrix diag(${diag}).`);
    }
    return lines.join('\n');
  }

  const mSel = $('#et-M'), nSel = $('#et-N'), out = $('#et-out');

  function render(){
    const Mk = mSel.value, Nk = nSel.value;
    const specM = SPECS[Mk], specN = SPECS[Nk];
    const Mnm = NAMES[Mk], Nnm = NAMES[Nk];

    const tor0 = combine(specM, specN, 'tensor');
    const tor1 = combine(specM, specN, 'tor1');
    const hom  = combine(specM, specN, 'hom');
    const ext1 = combine(specM, specN, 'ext1');

    const lines = [];
    lines.push(resolutionDesc(specM, Mnm));
    lines.push('');
    lines.push(`Apply −⊗ ${Nnm} (for Tor):`);
    lines.push(`   Tor_0(${Mnm}, ${Nnm}) = ${Mnm} ⊗ ${Nnm} = ${formatResult(tor0)}`);
    lines.push(`   Tor_1(${Mnm}, ${Nnm})                 = ${formatResult(tor1)}`);
    lines.push(`   Tor_i(·,·) = 0 for i ≥ 2 (ℤ has global dim 1).`);
    lines.push('');
    lines.push(`Apply Hom(−, ${Nnm}) (for Ext):`);
    lines.push(`   Ext^0(${Mnm}, ${Nnm}) = Hom(${Mnm}, ${Nnm}) = ${formatResult(hom)}`);
    lines.push(`   Ext^1(${Mnm}, ${Nnm})                  = ${formatResult(ext1)}`);
    lines.push(`   Ext^i(·,·) = 0 for i ≥ 2.`);

    out.textContent = lines.join('\n');
    out.style.color = 'var(--ink)';
  }
  mSel.addEventListener('change', render);
  nSel.addEventListener('change', render);
  render();
})();
</script>

10. Resolutions: projective, injective, flat

A projective resolution of an $A$-module $M$ is an exact sequence

$\cdots \to P_2 \to P_1 \to P_0 \twoheadrightarrow M \to 0$

in which every $P_i$ is projective (a direct summand of a free module). An injective resolution dually has $M \hookrightarrow I^0 \to I^1 \to \cdots$ exact with each $I^i$ injective. Flat resolutions use modules that are flat (tensor product with them preserves exact sequences). Free ⇒ projective ⇒ flat; over nice rings all three are distinct.

Why do resolutions always exist? In a category with enough projectives (every object is the quotient of a projective) you can iterate: $M$ is a quotient of some projective $P_0$; call the kernel $K_0$; it is a quotient of some projective $P_1$; continue. Same construction, dualized, works if there are enough injectives. The category $\mathsf{Ab}$ of abelian groups has both: projective = free (from a generating set) and injective = divisible (every abelian group embeds into a divisible one like $\prod \mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}$).

Over $\mathbb{Z}$, every finitely generated abelian group $M$ has a two-step free resolution $0 \to \mathbb{Z}^r \to \mathbb{Z}^s \to M \to 0$. The map $\mathbb{Z}^r \to \mathbb{Z}^s$ is a presentation matrix; its Smith normal form reads off the invariant factor decomposition $M \cong \mathbb{Z}^{s-r} \oplus \bigoplus \mathbb{Z}/d_i$.

Free resolution builder (finitely generated abelian groups)
add cyclic summands · see presentation matrix and Smith normal form

The finitely generated case over $\mathbb{Z}$ is misleadingly clean. Over $\mathbb{Z}[x]$ a free resolution of $\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2)$ already has length $1$ but is infinite; over $\mathbb{Z}[x,y]$ things can get arbitrarily long. The global dimension of a ring is the supremum of projective-resolution lengths over all modules. For fields it is $0$; for PIDs it is $\le 1$; for polynomial rings in $n$ variables over a field it is $n$ (Hilbert syzygy theorem).

<script>
/* =============================== SECTION 5: Free resolution builder =============================== */
(function(){
  const viewSpan = $('#res-view'), out = $('#res-out');
  const btns = $$('#w-res button[data-add]');
  const clear = $('#res-clear');

  let summands = [6, 0]; // default: Z/6 ⊕ Z

  function format(sum){
    if(sum.length===0) return '0';
    const parts = [];
    const free = sum.filter(x=>x===0).length;
    if(free>0) parts.push(free===1?'ℤ':`ℤ^${free}`);
    const cy = sum.filter(x=>x>0);
    for(const c of cy) parts.push(`ℤ/${c}`);
    return parts.join(' ⊕ ');
  }
  function render(){
    viewSpan.textContent = format(summands);
    // Build presentation matrix: rows = generators of M (= one per summand, length s).
    // For a summand Z/m_i, the relation m_i · e_i = 0 contributes a column (0,...,m_i,...,0).
    // For a summand Z, no relation.
    const s = summands.length;
    const cyclic = summands.map((m,i)=>({m,i})).filter(x=>x.m>0);
    const r = cyclic.length;
    // matrix is s×r (rows=s, cols=r). Column i of matrix has m_i at row i (the one corresponding to that cyclic).
    // But if free summands are mixed in, the row indices differ. Use position ordering of summands.
    const M = [];
    for(let a=0;a<s;a++){
      const row = new Array(r).fill(0);
      M.push(row);
    }
    cyclic.forEach((c,k)=>{ M[c.i][k] = c.m; });
    // Smith normal form for verification
    const invs = r===0? [] : smithNormalForm(M);
    // Number of Z summands (from the resolution's cokernel)
    const freeRank = s - r;
    // Now, invs are the nontrivial invariant factors of the torsion part.
    // Smith output: list of nonzero diagonal entries; 1's mean trivial; nonzero mean torsion factor.

    const lines = [];
    if(s===0){
      lines.push('M = 0');
      lines.push('trivial resolution.');
      out.textContent = lines.join('\n');
      return;
    }
    lines.push(`chosen summands: ${summands.length===0?'(empty)':summands.map(m=>m===0?'ℤ':`ℤ/${m}`).join(', ')}`);
    lines.push(`s = ${s} (generators),  r = ${r} (relations)`);
    lines.push('');
    lines.push(`Two-step free resolution:`);
    lines.push(r===0
      ? `   0  →  ℤ^${s}  →  M  →  0`
      : `   0  →  ℤ^${r}  ─A→  ℤ^${s}  →  M  →  0`);
    if(r>0){
      lines.push('');
      lines.push('Presentation matrix A (columns are relations):');
      for(const row of M) lines.push('   [  ' + row.map(x=>String(x).padStart(3,' ')).join('  ') + '  ]');
    }
    lines.push('');
    lines.push(`Smith invariant factors: [${invs.join(', ')}]`);
    lines.push(`Recovered M ≅ ${zmoduleFromSmith(invs, freeRank)}`);

    out.textContent = lines.join('\n');
  }
  btns.forEach(b=>{
    b.addEventListener('click', ()=>{ summands.push(parseInt(b.dataset.add,10)); render(); });
  });
  clear.addEventListener('click', ()=>{ summands = []; render(); });
  render();

</script>

11. Spectral sequences (a gentle taste)

A spectral sequence is a sequence of "pages" $E_0, E_1, E_2, \dots$ where each page is a bigraded abelian group with a differential $d_r: E_r^{p,q} \to E_r^{p-r, q+r-1}$ (or with indices reversed, depending on convention) satisfying $d_r^2 = 0$, and the next page is the homology of the current one: $E_{r+1}^{p,q} = H(E_r^{p,q}, d_r)$. Under reasonable finiteness conditions, the differentials eventually vanish and the pages stabilize at an $E_\infty$ page, which is (up to a filtration) the answer you were after.

Two canonical examples:

  1. Filtered complex. Given a filtration $\cdots\subseteq F_p C \subseteq F_{p+1}C\subseteq \cdots$ on a chain complex, there is a spectral sequence with $E_0^{p,q} = F_pC_{p+q}/F_{p-1}C_{p+q}$ converging to $H_*(C)$.
  2. Double complex. A bigraded object $C^{p,q}$ with horizontal and vertical differentials $d_h, d_v$ satisfying $d_h^2 = d_v^2 = 0$ and $d_hd_v + d_vd_h = 0$ has total complex $\mathrm{Tot}^n = \bigoplus_{p+q=n} C^{p,q}$ with differential $d_h + d_v$. The two filtrations (by rows vs. by columns) give two spectral sequences, both converging to $H_*(\mathrm{Tot})$.

The double complex spectral sequence is the main application of "spectral sequences are easy." Example: for right-derived bifunctors, a Grothendieck spectral sequence of the form $E_2^{p,q} = R^p F(R^q G (X)) \Rightarrow R^{p+q}(FG)(X)$ organizes iterated derived functors. The Leray spectral sequence for $Y\to X$ and the Lyndon–Hochschild–Serre spectral sequence for $1\to N\to G\to Q\to 1$ are both special cases.

Double-complex spectral sequence pages
a 2×2 double complex with horizontal × 2 and vertical × 3 · click "next page" to advance

The spectral sequence of a double complex is an essential bookkeeping device, but its computational power is modest: it tells you that the totalization's homology is some extension of the limit entries by each other, not exactly what that extension is. Extension problems are the frustration tax of the subject.
</section>

/* =============================== SECTION 6: Spectral sequence viewer =============================== */
(function(){

  // E_2 page: cohomology of d_1.

  //
  // Let's sanity check: totalization of the 2×2 double complex in degrees 0,1,2.
  //   Tot^0 = C^{0,0} = Z
  //   Tot^1 = C^{1,0} ⊕ C^{0,1} = Z^2

  // Then H^0 = ker d^0 = 0  (since (2,3) is injective).
  //      H^1 = ker d^1 / im d^0 = {(a,b) : 3a = 2b} / <(2,3)>.
  //        solutions: a = 2t, b = 3t. So ker d^1 = Z·(2,3) = im d^0. H^1 = 0.
  //      H^2 = coker d^1 = Z / (3Z + 2Z) = Z / Z = 0.
  // Yes, total cohomology is zero. Spectral sequence converges to 0. ✓

  const svg = $('#ss-svg'), out = $('#ss-out'), label = $('#ss-label');
  const prev = $('#ss-prev'), next = $('#ss-next');
  let page = 0;

        ['ℤ','ℤ'],   // row q=0 (bottom)
      ],
      differentials: {dir:'vertical', label:'d_0 = ·3 (vertical)', color:'var(--yellow)'}
    },
    {
      name: 'E_1',
      desc: 'Take vertical cohomology. Each column is 0 → ℤ —·3→ ℤ → 0. H^0 (ker ·3 on ℤ) = 0. H^1 (coker) = ℤ/3. So the E_1 page has ℤ/3 on the top row, 0 on the bottom row. New differentials d_1 = d_h are horizontal, multiplication by 2, and act as ℤ/3 →·2→ ℤ/3.',

        ['ℤ/3','ℤ/3'],
        ['0','0'],

  prev.addEventListener('click',()=>{page = Math.max(0, page-1); draw();});
  next.addEventListener('click',()=>{page = Math.min(PAGES.length-1, page+1); draw();});

12. Abelian categories and a triangulated preview

Every diagram chase in §§1–11 secretly takes place in an abelian category: a category with a zero object, all finite biproducts, all kernels and cokernels, and in which every monomorphism is a kernel and every epimorphism is a cokernel (the "every mono/epi is normal" axiom). In an abelian category, short exact sequences, the snake lemma, the five lemma, Ext/Tor, and all the tools we've built work verbatim.

Freyd–Mitchell embedding. Every small abelian category $\mathcal{A}$ admits a fully faithful, exact embedding $\mathcal{A}\hookrightarrow R\text{-Mod}$ for some ring $R$. Upshot: any element-level diagram chase in $\mathsf{Ab}$ transfers immediately to any abelian category — "you can do homological algebra without ever leaving modules."

Canonical examples of abelian categories:

  • $R$-Mod for any ring $R$ (left or right modules). The prototypical example.
  • Sheaves of abelian groups on a topological space (kernels computed sectionwise; cokernels require sheafification).
  • Chain complexes $\operatorname{Ch}(\mathcal{A})$ in an abelian category $\mathcal{A}$ — itself abelian, with pointwise kernels/cokernels.
  • Finitely generated modules over a Noetherian ring (abelian; closedness under kernel/cokernel uses Noetherian-ness).

The next conceptual leap is the derived category $D(\mathcal{A})$: take $\operatorname{Ch}(\mathcal{A})$, formally invert all quasi-isomorphisms, and work in the resulting category. It is not abelian — it is a triangulated category. Short exact sequences of complexes lift to distinguished triangles $X\to Y\to Z\xrightarrow{+1} X[1]$, and rotation/long-exact-sequence relations now follow from the triangulated axioms directly.

The fundamental identification: for $M,N\in\mathcal{A}$ viewed as complexes in degree $0$, $$\operatorname{Hom}_{D(\mathcal{A})}(M, N[i]) \;\cong\; \operatorname{Ext}^i_\mathcal{A}(M, N).$$ Every Ext group appears as a hom in the derived category after a shift. This is why modern homological algebra is written in derived/$\infty$-categorical language.

Category classifier: abelian, triangulated, or neither?
pick a category, see which axioms hold

Where this goes next

What we've skipped or only previewed, in order of increasing sophistication:

  • The derived category, fleshed out. Instead of computing $\operatorname{Ext}$ and $\operatorname{Tor}$ one degree at a time, invert all quasi-isomorphisms of complexes and work in the resulting triangulated category $D(\mathsf{Ab})$. A map in $D(\mathsf{Ab})$ between two objects $M, N$ placed in degree zero is precisely $\operatorname{Ext}^0(M,N)$; a degree-$i$ map is $\operatorname{Ext}^i(M,N)$. Long exact sequences become distinguished triangles.
  • Sheaf cohomology. Apply the same machine to sheaves of abelian groups on a topological space. The global sections functor is left exact; its right derived functors $H^i(X,\mathcal{F}) := R^i\Gamma(\mathcal{F})$ are sheaf cohomology. Čech cohomology is a concrete model for this.
  • Group cohomology. $H^n(G,M) := \operatorname{Ext}^n_{\mathbb{Z}G}(\mathbb{Z}, M)$. It governs extensions of $G$ by $M$, obstructions to lifting actions, and the characteristic classes of central extensions.
  • Koszul duality and non-abelian derived functors. Replace $\mathsf{Ab}$ by more exotic categories — DG categories, $\infty$-categories, $\mathbb{E}_\infty$-algebras — and derived functors become Quillen-type functors between model categories. This is the setting for modern derived algebraic geometry and the work of Lurie and others.

The strange thing about homological algebra is that it started as a computation technique — Poincaré counting holes in surfaces — and ended up as the grammar in which nearly all modern algebraic geometry, representation theory, and algebraic topology is written. The derived functor is the word "and," everywhere.
